evidence.text: Company: Atlas Medical Systems2
Website:
Industry: Medical Device Manufacturing
Question: Why is product development slowing down even though every team appears to be working hard?
Context:
Context
Atlas has grown rapidly over the past three years.
Engineering, Regulatory, Manufacturing, Sales, Customer Support, and Product Management all believe they are executing well.
Despite this, product launches continue slipping and customer issues remain unresolved for weeks.
Leadership suspects the problem is systemic rather than individual performance.
Evidence
Engineering says requirements frequently change after development has already begun.
Manufacturing often discovers design problems only after production planning has started.
Customer Support learns about product releases from customers before internal announcements.
Product Managers say every department optimizes for its own goals rather than overall product success.
Teams rely heavily on informal Slack conversations because official documentation is outdated.
Engineers frequently recreate analyses because previous project decisions cannot be located.
Multiple departments maintain their own versions of the same product specifications.
Critical customer issues require meetings involving six different departments before anyone can act.
Project ownership changes several times during development.
Retrospectives identify the same coordination problems quarter after quarter with little lasting improvement.
Managers report that everyone is busy, yet no one feels responsible for the entire product lifecycle.
